metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either inline the relevant T4 safety plan constraints directly in the plan or add a link/reference that resolves to a real document, and add an AC that verifies adherence (e.g., 'T4 safety checklist completed and signed off').
evidence: 'What — irreversible; execute under the T4 safety plan' — 'T4 safety plan' appears only once and is never defined.
No AC verifies adherence to the T4 safety plan.
No link or inline summary of the T4 safety plan is provided.
findings.finding: The What section instructs execution 'under the T4 safety plan' but no such plan is defined, linked, or summarized anywhere in the document. This creates a cross-section inconsistency: the approach is constrained by an external artifact that is invisible to the implementer and unverified by any AC.
findings.location: What section header; AC section (no corresponding AC)
scenarios: Implementer does not know what constraints the T4 safety plan imposes; may skip safety steps believing the plan is self-contained.
Reviewer cannot attest that the T4 safety plan was followed because it is undefined.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism for 'bridge-bot-authored' identification in the cleanup script — clarify whether it uses (a) RECONCILER_MARKER presence in the body, (b) the Jira author accountId matching the JIRA_USER account, or (c) both — since no such filter exists in the current codebase and the plan does not define one.
evidence: outbound_comments.py:43: RECONCILER_MARKER = '<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->' — the existing mechanism for identifying bot comments is body content, not author field.
outbound_comments.py:150-183: _is_machine_marker_comment() filters by body prefix (BRIDGE_CANARY_ALERT:, 'Still stale as of'), not author.
Search for 'bridge-bot' in the codebase returns only docs/jira-sync-setup.md (example username) — no author-based filter logic exists.
Search for 'bot author filter' and 'comment author' in reconciler source returns no matches.
The Jira comment author accountId is available in the comment payload (acli.py:586-590 shows accountId resolution), but no code exists to use it for deletion filtering.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'only delete bridge-bot-authored comments (never human comments)' as a safety invariant, but no mechanism for identifying 'bridge-bot-authored' comments by Jira author field is present in the existing codebase or specified in the plan. The existing code identifies its own outbound comments by the presence of RECONCILER_MARKER (`<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->`) in the comment body — not by author. The plan's author-based filter is an asserted-but-unspecified mechanism that the cleanup script must implement from scratch.
findings.location: What section — 'only delete bridge-bot-authored comments (never human comments)'
scenarios: The cleanup script uses only body-normalization dedup without author filtering — it could delete duplicate human comments that have the same body text.
The script implements author filtering but uses the wrong accountId for the bot user, silently skipping all deletions or deleting the wrong set.
findings.suggested_fix: Use RECONCILER_MARKER presence as the primary bot-authored signal (matching the existing codebase logic), with a secondary check that the comment author's emailAddress matches JIRA_USER — and document that pre-marker-era bot comments (created before the marker was introduced) may be missed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit pagination loop requirement to the backup and fetch steps: the script MUST iterate over all pages (`startAt` / `maxResults`) until `isLast: true` (or equivalent) to retrieve all comments before computing the delete set, and the backup artifact must contain the total comment count for verification.
evidence: Jira Cloud REST `GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comments` returns a paginated result with a default `maxResults` far below 5000; fetching all ~5000 comments requires a `startAt`/`maxResults` loop.
Plan says 'export FULL comment payload' but never mentions pagination — an implementer following the plan literally would call the endpoint once and receive only the first page.
The dedup logic ('keep OLDEST (lowest created)') requires access to ALL comments to determine which is oldest; a partial fetch could keep a non-oldest comment, deleting the true canonical.
findings.finding: The plan never specifies how to paginate the Jira comment fetch. Jira Cloud's REST API returns at most 50-100 comments per page by default (the `maxResults` parameter is capped well below 5000). For tickets with ~5000 comments, fetching only the first page of results produces a critically incomplete backup and a wrong delete list — the vast majority of duplicates would be missed. This is a silent implementer assumption with no fallback.
findings.location: What section — 'Backup: export FULL comment payload (id, body, author, created) for all 13 tickets'; 'Keep-one dedup: for each distinct normalized body, keep the OLDEST'
scenarios: Script calls GET /rest/api/3/issue/REB-155/comments without pagination and receives the first 50 of 5000 comments; backup is 1% complete; delete list is constructed from an unrepresentative sample; most duplicates are left uncleaned.
Script keeps the 'oldest' comment visible in the first page, but the true oldest comment is on page 50; the canonical is deleted and a duplicate survives.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Backup step: 'Fetch ALL comments using pagination: iterate GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comments?startAt=0&maxResults=100 until startAt + returned count >= total; verify backup comment count matches the Jira-reported total before proceeding.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the 'normalized body' algorithm concretely in the plan: specify whether bodies are compared as raw ADF JSON, rendered text, or a specific field (e.g., the `text` leaf of each ADF paragraph node); state the normalization steps (e.g., strip leading/trailing whitespace from each text node, ignore ADF metadata fields); and add a dry-run AC that verifies the normalization correctly identifies the known duplicates on at least one of the 13 tickets (spot-check REB-155's 76-local / 5000-Jira count).
evidence: Plan uses 'distinct normalized body' as the dedup key but never defines the normalization function (e.g., strip leading/trailing whitespace? collapse internal whitespace? strip Jira ADF markup? lowercase? hash after normalization?).
Jira Cloud stores comment bodies in Atlassian Document Format (ADF); the body returned by the REST API is a JSON structure, not plain text — 'normalization' of ADF is a non-trivial design decision.
The pre-P1 bug re-posted identical comments; the body field in Jira REST responses may or may not be byte-identical depending on whether Jira normalizes storage — this is unverified in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan's core dedup predicate — 'each distinct normalized body' — never defines what 'normalized' means. Normalization strategy is load-bearing: if Jira's markup/storage introduces whitespace, newlines, or HTML encoding differences between otherwise-identical bot comments, an insufficiently-specified normalizer would fail to recognize duplicates (leaving thousands uncleaned); an over-aggressive normalizer could incorrectly merge genuinely distinct comments. The plan provides no algorithm, no examples, and no AC requiring verification that the normalization correctly identifies the confirmed duplicates on these tickets.
findings.location: What section — 'Keep-one dedup: for each distinct normalized body, keep the OLDEST (lowest created), delete the rest'
scenarios: Jira ADF introduces a timestamp or revision field inside the body JSON that differs between copies of the same comment; naive body comparison treats each as distinct, keeping all 5000 'duplicates'.
Normalization strips too aggressively; two distinct comments with slightly different content are merged; the canonical is deleted and a different comment survives.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the pagination mechanism for fetching ALL comments from capped tickets (e.g., reuse `acli.py::get_comments` with `--paginate` + `_parse_paginated_comments`, which is live-validated for 5000-comment issues) so the backup and keep-oldest selection operate on the complete set.
evidence: Plan states 'export FULL comment payload' without specifying how to paginate Jira's comment-list endpoint for 5000-comment tickets
acli_cli_ops.py::_parse_paginated_comments docstring: 'a 5000-comment issue yields ~101 back-to-back {"comments": [...]} objects'
acli.py::get_comments: '--paginate is REQUIRED (bug 1f3d): without it ACLI returns only the first page (50, oldest), so the outbound dedup re-posts everything past page 1'
The plan does not reference get_comments, --paginate, or _parse_paginated_comments as the enumeration mechanism
ABSENCE: no pagination strategy (startAt/maxResults loop or ACLI --paginate) is named for the backup fetch step
findings.finding: The plan's backup/enumeration step asserts it will 'export FULL comment payload (id, body, author, created) for all 13 tickets' — but Jira Cloud's comment-list endpoint returns a limited page of results and requires explicit pagination to retrieve all 5000 comments from a capped ticket. The plan does not specify a pagination strategy, nor does it reference the repo's existing live-validated solution (acli.py::get_comments with --paginate + _parse_paginated_comments, which the codebase explicitly notes handles '5000-comment issue yields ~101 back-to-back JSON objects'). Without this, the backup and the keep-oldest selection step may silently operate on an incomplete comment set, potentially deleting the wrong comments (e.g., the true oldest comment may be on page 2 and not fetched).
findings.location: What — 'Backup: export FULL comment payload (id, body, author, created) for all 13 tickets to a versioned JSON artifact BEFORE any deletion.'
scenarios: Backup script uses a naive single GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment call (default page size 50 or 100), captures only the first page of 5000 comments, misidentifies the 'oldest' comment, and deletes the true canonical comment while keeping a duplicate
findings.suggested_fix: Reuse the existing `AcliClient.get_comments(jira_key)` (or the standalone `get_comments` in `acli_cli_ops.py`) for the enumeration phase — it already handles 5000-comment ticket pagination via `--paginate` and `_parse_paginated_comments`. Reference it explicitly in the plan's backup step to close the unverified-capability gap.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the normalization algorithm for 'body' comparison: specify (a) which Jira API response field and media type to use (e.g., `body` as plain text via `text/plain` Accept header, or ADF-to-text extraction), (b) any whitespace/unicode normalization step, and (c) the hash function applied to the normalized form — so any implementation produces the same grouping and the log's `body hash` is reproducible.
evidence: Plan uses 'normalized body' as the dedup key without defining what normalization means

Jira Cloud comments may be returned as ADF JSON or markdown depending on the Accept header and API version; byte-level comparison of two nominally-identical comments will fail unless a canonical form is specified
The log records 'body hash' — the hash function and the pre-hash normalization step are both unspecified, making the log non-reproducible across runs
ABSENCE: no definition of normalization algorithm, canonical form, or comparison function appears anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The dedup key 'distinct normalized body' is the central grouping criterion for the entire deletion operation, but 'normalized' is never defined. Jira comments in Cloud can be stored as Atlassian Document Format (ADF) objects or as markdown strings; trailing whitespace, newline encoding, and unicode normalization can produce distinct byte representations of semantically identical bodies. Without a normalization spec (e.g., 'strip leading/trailing whitespace, collapse internal runs of whitespace, compare as plain text after ADF-to-text extraction'), two implementations will produce different groupings and different delete lists — for an irreversible operation on 13 capped tickets.
findings.location: What section — 'Keep-one dedup: for each distinct normalized body, keep the OLDEST (lowest created), delete the rest'
scenarios: Script A strips whitespace and compares plain text; script B compares raw ADF JSON bytes — they produce different delete-set sizes for the same ticket, one over-deletes
A comment body has a trailing newline in one copy and not in another (Jira storage artifact); without normalization they are treated as distinct, leaving one duplicate alive
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Normalization' definition: fetch comments with `Accept: text/plain` (or extract ADF `content[].content[].text` recursively), strip leading/trailing whitespace, and SHA-256 the UTF-8 encoded result. Use this hash as the dedup key and the log's `body hash` field.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact Jira `accountId` (or verified `emailAddress`) of the bridge-bot account on navasage.atlassian.net so the author filter is unambiguous and robust to display-name changes; add it to the Auth section or the dedup bullet.
evidence: Plan says 'only delete bridge-bot-authored comments' but never specifies which Jira accountId, email, or display name corresponds to 'bridge-bot'
docs/jira-sync-setup.md:60 shows `bridge-bot@your-org.com` as a placeholder example user, not the actual accountId used in navasage.atlassian.net

Jira Cloud REST API comment objects return `author.accountId` (a non-guessable UUID), `author.displayName`, and `author.emailAddress` — filtering by display name is fragile (renameable); filtering by accountId requires the actual value
ABSENCE: no `accountId`, `emailAddress`, or resolved display name for the bridge-bot Jira account appears in the plan or in any referenced artifact
findings.finding: The safety-critical filter 'only delete bridge-bot-authored comments (never human comments)' references 'bridge-bot' as an identity, but no Jira accountId, display name, or email is defined for this actor. In Jira Cloud, comment authors are identified by `author.accountId` (a UUID), not display names, which can be changed or shared. An implementer has no authoritative value to filter on, making the human-comment-protection guarantee unenforceable without out-of-band knowledge.
findings.location: What section — 'only delete bridge-bot-authored comments (never human comments). Jira REST DELETE /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment/{id}'
scenarios: Implementer filters by displayName 'Bridge Bot' but the actual Jira account uses a different display name — human-comment protection silently fails, and human comments are deleted
A future Jira account rename changes the displayName; the filter stops working mid-run and deletes human comments on later tickets
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Auth section: 'Bridge-bot Jira accountId: <UUID from GET /rest/api/3/myself while authenticated as the bot account>. Filter deletions to comments where `comment.author.accountId == <that UUID>`.' Include a dry-run assertion that every comment in the delete list has this accountId.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that explicitly verifies no human-authored comments were deleted, e.g., 'Deletion log shows zero non-bridge-bot author ids; confirmed by cross-referencing log against backup artifact,' and ensure the deletion log captures the author field.
evidence: 'only delete bridge-bot-authored comments (never human comments)' — stated in What, not covered by any AC.
AC 2 verifies only that tickets are below cap and sync is restored; AC 3 verifies only that a deletion log exists.
Deletion log captures 'ticket, comment id, body hash' — author field is not logged, making post-hoc verification of this constraint impossible from the log alone.
findings.finding: The keep-one dedup logic includes a critical constraint — 'only delete bridge-bot-authored comments (never human comments)' — but no AC verifies this property was respected post-execution. An operator-attested count-below-cap check does not confirm that zero human comments were deleted.
findings.location: What section, keep-one dedup bullet; AC section (no covering criterion)
scenarios: A bug in the author-filter deletes human comments; the operator-attested AC 2 passes because the ticket is below cap; the violation goes undetected.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to deletion log schema: author_account_id. Add AC: 'Zero non-bridge-bot comment deletions confirmed by auditing author_account_id field in deletion log against bridge-bot account id.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state and hard-code the bridge bot's Jira `accountId` (not email/username) in the cleanup script; add a pre-flight step that confirms the known accountId matches the JIRA_USER credential's actual account (via GET /rest/api/3/myself) before any deletions execute.
evidence: Plan: 'only delete bridge-bot-authored comments (never human comments)' — no accountId or email named
ABSENCE: search for 'bridge-bot author comment author_account_id' → no hits in codebase
ABSENCE: no constant like BRIDGE_BOT_ACCOUNT_ID or JIRA_USER pattern for bot identification in acli_graph.py, acli.py, or acli_cli_ops.py
The JIRA_USER env var is the bot's username for auth, but the plan doesn't state whether to match on this value in comment author fields — and Jira Cloud comments carry accountId, not username
Unverified: whether the bridge bot's Jira accountId is the same as the JIRA_USER env value or requires a separate lookup
findings.finding: The plan states 'only delete bridge-bot-authored comments (never human comments)' but does not specify the accountId, emailAddress, or displayName used to identify the bridge bot in the Jira comment author field. No such identifier constant exists in the codebase (searched for 'bridge-bot author accountId' — no hits). Without a pinned, verified bot account identifier, the author filter is an unverified integration surface for this new cleanup script: a wrong or missing value could silently match zero comments (deleting nothing) or allow human comments through.
findings.location: What section — 'only delete bridge-bot-authored comments (never human comments)'
scenarios: The cleanup script uses JIRA_USER (email) to match comment authors, but Jira Cloud comment author objects carry accountId (opaque UUID), not email — filter matches nothing and 0 comments are deleted
The accountId check is omitted or wrong: the script deletes human comments on shared tickets, causing data loss on an irreversible operation
findings.suggested_fix: Add a preflight call to GET /rest/api/3/myself using the JIRA_API_TOKEN credentials; record the returned `accountId`; use that value as the exclusive author filter on all comment deletions. Document the expected accountId in the plan for auditability.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add AC lines that mechanically verify the dedup predicates: (a) script emits a summary of human-authored comments found and excluded (expected: 0 deletions of human comments), and (b) for a random sample of ≥5 kept/deleted pairs per ticket, assert kept.created < all(deleted.created) — these checks must appear in the dry-run output and be reviewable in seconds, not minutes.
evidence: Plan states: 'Dry-run FIRST: emit the exact per-ticket delete list (comment ids + kept canonical) and show the diff for human approval before --execute'
Plan states: 'Backup artifact + dry-run diff produced and human-approved BEFORE deletion' — sole pre-execution gate
At ~4,900 deletes/ticket × 13 tickets, diff volume makes line-by-line human verification of sort-order and author correctness infeasible
No AC requires a machine-verifiable summary (e.g., count of human comments excluded, spot-check that kept.created < min(deleted.created) for a sample)
findings.finding: The dry-run approval AC is not a credible behavioral test of the two core dedup predicates — (a) 'keep oldest created' and (b) 'bot-authored only' — because at ~4,900 deletions × 13 tickets (~63,700 lines), human review is a rubber stamp rather than meaningful verification; if the script has a sorting bug or incorrectly classifies author, the approval passes while the wrong comments are deleted.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC line 1 / What section: 'Keep-one dedup' bullet
scenarios: Script has an off-by-one in created-timestamp sort: keeps a duplicate instead of the canonical oldest — human approves without noticing across 63K lines
Script author-filter has a regex bug: one human comment is in the delete list — humans miss it in the volume
Post-run, a canonical comment is gone and the wrong duplicate survives — no AC catches this until someone manually reads Jira
findings.suggested_fix: In the dry-run output, add a machine-generated summary section per ticket: total_found, distinct_bodies, human_comments_skipped (must be 0 for bot-only assertion), oldest_kept_ids[], and for each kept id: kept_created vs max_deleted_created. Add an AC: 'Dry-run summary shows human_comments_skipped=0 for all 13 tickets and kept.created < all sibling deleted.created for every kept comment (script asserts this and fails loudly if violated).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a default API call rate / throttle in the plan (e.g., 100 ms inter-call delay or ≤10 req/s) and specify that on HTTP 429 the script waits `Retry-After` seconds before resuming, so an executor has a concrete default rather than guessing.
evidence: ~4,900+ duplicates per ticket × 13 tickets = ~63,700 deletion calls total; no throttle, retry-after, or backoff strategy mentioned
Plan says 'Idempotent + resumable' but does not define how rate-limit 429s are handled or re-entered
findings.finding: The plan makes ~63,700 Jira REST DELETE calls (~4,900+ per ticket × 13 tickets) with no stated throttle or rate-limit strategy, even though Jira Cloud enforces API rate limits. Without a stated default (e.g., 100 ms inter-call delay or 10 req/s cap), an executing agent has no basis for choosing a rate, and mid-run 429 errors will silently abort batches in a non-resumable way unless the logging/retry logic is also specified.
findings.location: What section — 'Jira REST DELETE /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment/{id}'
scenarios: Jira Cloud returns HTTP 429 mid-run; script aborts; backup is complete but partial deletions leave tickets in an inconsistent state and re-running requires knowing the resume point
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'Throttle: ≤10 DELETE requests/second (100 ms sleep between calls); on HTTP 429 respect the Retry-After header and retry; the resumable log must record each deletion atomically so a restart skips already-deleted IDs.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit retry-with-exponential-backoff (e.g., up to 5 retries, initial 1 s delay, respect Retry-After header on 429) for every Jira DELETE and GET call, and document the rate-limit handling strategy in the script.
evidence: 'Idempotent + resumable; log each deletion (ticket, comment id, body hash)' — no retry, backoff, or 429-handling mentioned
~63,000 total DELETE calls across 13 tickets will almost certainly trigger Jira Cloud rate limits
No timeout, circuit-breaker, or sleep/backoff policy specified
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'idempotent + resumable' but specifies no retry/backoff strategy for Jira API rate limits or transient failures during mass deletion (~4,900 DELETEs per ticket × 13 tickets ≈ 63,000+ calls). Jira Cloud enforces rate limits; without explicit retry/backoff the script will receive 429s, fail mid-ticket, and leave tickets partially cleaned — undermining the resumability guarantee.
findings.location: What section — 'Idempotent + resumable; log each deletion'
scenarios: Script hits Jira rate limit at comment 200 of 4900 on REB-155; throws unhandled exception; ticket left at 4800 duplicates — new comments still fail to sync
Re-run skips already-deleted IDs (404) but still has no rate-limit protection, so the same failure recurs
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap each DELETE (and bulk GET) in a retry loop: catch HTTP 429 and 5xx, sleep for `Retry-After` seconds (or exponential backoff starting at 1 s), retry up to 5 times, then surface the error and halt cleanly so the log captures the last successful deletion and a re-run can resume from that point.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit rate-limiting/batching to the delete script (e.g., configurable delay between requests and/or max-requests-per-minute cap) to handle Jira Cloud API rate limits across ~64k deletions.
evidence: 'Purging ~4900+ duplicate comments per ticket' with no mention of request rate, sleep/backoff, or batch size
'Idempotent + resumable; log each deletion' — resumability is specified but throttling is not
Jira Cloud REST API enforces rate limits (typically 10 req/s per token)
findings.finding: The plan targets ~4,900+ deletions per ticket × 13 tickets (~64k+ Jira REST DELETE calls) but specifies no batching, throttling, or rate-limit handling. An unthrottled sweep risks hitting Jira API rate limits mid-run, producing partial failures that are hard to diagnose and recover from even with resumability in place.
findings.location: What — 'purging ~4900+ duplicate comments per ticket'
scenarios: Script issues 64k deletes at full speed, hits 429 rate-limit, crashes mid-ticket; operator must diagnose which deletions succeeded before resuming
Burst of deletes triggers temporary token suspension, blocking the reconciler as well
findings.suggested_fix: Add a --rate-limit N (requests/minute, default 300) flag and a per-request sleep; on HTTP 429 response, back off with exponential retry before resuming from the logged checkpoint.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add 429-aware backoff to the cleanup script's DELETE loop: on HTTP 429, sleep for the `Retry-After` header value (or a fixed 60 s) and retry; also add a configurable per-request inter-call delay (e.g. 50–100 ms) to stay under the Jira Cloud rate limit across ~63K calls.
evidence: Plan: '~4900+ duplicate comments per ticket' × 13 tickets ≈ 63,700+ REST DELETEs

src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/acli_rest.py:36-83: _rest_urlopen_with_retry retries on TimeoutError/ConnectionError only; does NOT catch urllib.error.HTTPError (line 62: 'HTTP errors (4xx/5xx) are deterministic — do not retry')
src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/acli_rest.py:304-319: _direct_rest_delete calls _rest_urlopen_with_retry with no 429 handling
.rebar/cache/plan-review/bd80-0603-7d44-4d9f/8ad5afb54353a9c70618.json: independently flagged 'no stated throttle or rate-limit strategy' against this same plan
Unverified: Jira Cloud rate-limit behaviour at this volume (training-time knowledge, not live-probed)
findings.finding: The plan issues ~63,700+ sequential REST DELETE calls (≈4,900+ per ticket × 13 tickets) but states no rate-limit strategy. The existing _direct_rest_delete/_rest_urlopen_with_retry retries only on TimeoutError/ConnectionError — NOT on HTTP 429 Too Many Requests. Jira Cloud enforces API rate limits; without a 429-aware backoff or inter-request delay, the cleanup script will fail mid-run (non-resumably unless the script re-checks which IDs were already deleted) and leave tickets partially purged.
findings.location: What section — 'Idempotent + resumable; log each deletion (ticket, comment id, body hash)'
scenarios: Script runs ~200 deletes/min before hitting Jira's rate limit, receives HTTP 429, raises HTTPError, script crashes mid-ticket with partial deletion and no safe resume point
Even if the script is 'resumable' via its deletion log, without detecting 429 it will retry the same request immediately rather than backing off, causing cascading failures
findings.suggested_fix: In the cleanup script's delete loop, wrap each DELETE in a retry block: catch `urllib.error.HTTPError` with `code == 429`, read the `Retry-After` header (default 60 s if absent), sleep, and retry. Also add `time.sleep(0.05)` between requests to reduce burst pressure. This is a new script (not modifying the existing AcliRestMixin), so it can implement its own retry loop independently.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete backup-validation step (e.g., `jq 'to_entries[] | {key: .key, backed_up: (.value|length)}' backup.json` cross-checked against the known Jira totals per ticket) that must pass before the dry-run diff is presented for approval.
evidence: What section: 'export FULL comment payload (id, body, author, created) for all 13 tickets to a versioned JSON artifact'
No verification command (e.g., `jq '.[] | {key, count: (.comments|length)}' backup.json`) is specified to confirm the backup matches the known Jira totals before --execute is run
The operation is explicitly tagged 'irreversible'
findings.finding: The plan prescribes an irreversible deletion run but provides no concrete proving command to verify the backup artifact's completeness and correctness before deletion begins. The AC only says 'backup artifact … produced and human-approved'; it does not specify how to confirm the backup captured all expected comments (e.g., a cardinality check comparing JSON count to Jira-reported total for each ticket).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Backup artifact + dry-run diff produced and human-approved BEFORE deletion'
scenarios: Backup script silently paginates incorrectly and captures only the first 1,000 comments; human approves assuming completeness; deletion proceeds; recovery is impossible without the missing comment data
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section after the backup bullet: 'Validate backup: for each of the 13 tickets assert `jq --arg key REB-155 '.[$key].comments | length' backup.json` equals the Jira-reported total (e.g., 5000); fail loudly if any ticket's count doesn't match before proceeding to dry-run.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out/restore procedure: describe how to use the backup JSON to re-POST deleted comments via Jira REST API (`POST /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment`) and under what conditions to invoke it.
evidence: 'What — irreversible; execute under the T4 safety plan'
Backup step specifies exporting JSON payload but no corresponding restore procedure is described anywhere in the plan.

T4 rubric: 'The remedy for a destructive/breaking change is an explicit ROLLBACK / back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing — checking only that breakage is acknowledged is insufficient; require the reversibility mechanism.'
findings.finding: The plan marks the operation 'irreversible' and references 'the T4 safety plan' as the governing safety framework, but no rollback or restore procedure is defined anywhere. A backup artifact is specified, but the plan never describes how to use it to recover (e.g., re-POST deleted comments via Jira REST API) if wrong comments are deleted or the script malfunctions mid-run. Per T4, acknowledgement of irreversibility is insufficient — an explicit back-out plan is required.
findings.location: What section header: 'irreversible; execute under the T4 safety plan'
scenarios: Script deletes wrong comment IDs due to a filter bug mid-run (partial execution); operator needs to restore — no procedure exists.
Human comment is accidentally matched and deleted; backup exists but no documented path to re-create it via API.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection under What: 'If incorrect deletions are detected, use the backup JSON to restore via POST /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment for each deleted comment id. Trigger if: (a) post-run comment count differs from expected by more than N, or (b) any non-bridge-bot comment id appears in the deletion log.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the non-happy-path operator paths: (a) explicit abandon/no-op exit after dry-run rejection, (b) how the deletion log is used on resume to skip already-completed ids, and (c) how transient API errors (429, auth expiry, 5xx) are handled (retry-with-backoff vs. abort).
evidence: 'Dry-run FIRST: emit the exact per-ticket delete list … and show the diff for human approval before --execute' — no explicit abandon/reject path described.
'Idempotent + resumable; log each deletion (ticket, comment id, body hash)' — does not state how the resume logic uses the log to skip already-deleted ids or how it handles a 429/auth-expiry mid-run.
Deletion is described as 'irreversible' — making partial-failure recovery the highest-stakes non-happy-path.
findings.finding: The operator interaction flow (dry-run → human approval → execute) does not specify non-happy-path handling for: (a) the operator explicitly rejecting/abandoning after reviewing the dry-run diff, (b) Jira API transient errors mid-deletion (rate-limit 429, auth token expiry, network timeout), or (c) partial completion detection on resume. 'Idempotent + resumable' is asserted but the mechanism for detecting which deletions already completed (to skip on retry) is unspecified, making the resume guarantee unverifiable.
findings.location: What — 'Dry-run FIRST' and 'Idempotent + resumable; log each deletion'
scenarios: Operator reviews dry-run, decides cleanup is premature, and has no documented way to formally abandon — leaves the process in an ambiguous state.

Script hits Jira rate limit (429) after deleting 2000 of 4900 comments on REB-155; without a defined resume mechanism (e.g., skip ids already in the deletion log), a retry re-attempts already-deleted ids and the operator cannot distinguish completed from pending work.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either link to / excerpt the T4 safety plan's concrete recovery procedure for Jira comment deletion, or add an explicit Rollback section: (a) acknowledge that original bot-author and created timestamps cannot be restored via Jira REST; (b) describe the best-available recovery (re-POST from backup JSON logs the restored content under the operator identity, with a note that provenance is approximate); and (c) define the trigger condition (e.g., any non-bridge-bot comment ID appears in the delete log → stop and investigate).
evidence: 'What — irreversible; execute under the T4 safety plan' — the T4 safety plan is referenced by name but never linked, excerpted, or summarized in the plan.
Backup step exports the full comment payload but no AC or step describes how to use it if wrong comments are deleted.

Jira REST POST /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment creates a new comment attributed to the calling user with the current timestamp — the original bot author and created timestamp cannot be restored via the standard API.
No AC requires verifying that the backup artifact can actually be used to restore (e.g., testing re-POST on a single comment before the main run).
findings.finding: The plan states the operation is 'irreversible' and directs execution 'under the T4 safety plan,' but it never specifies what the T4 safety plan's recovery procedure is, nor does it describe how to use the backup JSON artifact to restore deleted comments. The backup is the only named safety net, but POST /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment preserves the current author/timestamp, not the original bot-authored metadata — so re-posted comments would appear under the operator's identity and with the current timestamp, losing the original context. This makes the claimed safety plan incomplete as written.
findings.location: What section header: 'irreversible; execute under the T4 safety plan'; Backup bullet; Acceptance Criteria — 'Backup artifact + dry-run diff produced and human-approved BEFORE deletion'
scenarios: Script deletes a canonical comment due to a normalization bug; operator discovers the issue; backup JSON exists but re-posting creates a new comment under the operator's name with today's timestamp — original provenance is permanently lost.
T4 safety plan does not exist or applies to a different type of operation; operator has no recovery guidance.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the explicit rollback/restore procedure (including the restore script or API calls, handling of authorship/timestamp loss, and the post-rollback schema state) and attach a DATA LOSS RISK note acknowledging that re-POSTed comments cannot recover original author or created-timestamp fields.
evidence: 'Backup: export FULL comment payload (id, body, author, created)' — backup is specified
No 'restore from backup' script, procedure, or API call is described anywhere in the plan
Jira REST DELETE /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment/{id} has no undo endpoint
Re-POSTing comments via REST creates new comments with the bot's authorship/current timestamp, losing original author attribution and created timestamps — not a true restore
DATA LOSS RISK: original comment authorship and timestamps cannot be recovered via re-POST
findings.finding: Jira REST DELETE is irreversible. The plan mandates a backup JSON artifact but provides no documented restore procedure (no script, no API path, no handling of original authorship/timestamps). If a partial failure occurs or the wrong comments are deleted, recovery is undefined — DATA LOSS RISK.
findings.location: What — 'Backup: export FULL comment payload … BEFORE any deletion'; AC — 'Backup artifact … produced and human-approved BEFORE deletion'
scenarios: Script deletes 3000 comments on REB-155 then crashes; operator restores from backup via re-POST but comments appear under the bot account with today's timestamp, losing audit trail
Wrong normalization logic causes deletion of unique human-adjacent comments not caught by 'never delete human comments' guard
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Restore procedure' subsection: provide a restore script that re-POSTs deleted comments from the backup JSON (accepting that authorship/timestamps will be the bot's); explicitly note 'DATA LOSS RISK: original authorship and created timestamps are unrecoverable after deletion; restored comments will appear under the bot account'.

evidence: vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

evidence: file_impact is empty
findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `--execute` re-fetches the live comment list from Jira for each ticket and recomputes the delete set using the same dedup logic, rather than replaying the dry-run snapshot, and explicitly state that 404 responses on individual DELETEs are skipped silently (already-deleted = no-op, supporting idempotency).
evidence: Plan states 'Dry-run FIRST: emit the exact per-ticket delete list … for human approval before --execute' — no statement of whether --execute replays the dry-run ID list or re-fetches live state at execution time.
P1 (a6cdad0e2) stops NEW bot duplication, but human comments or edge-case bot retries between dry-run and --execute can alter the comment set.
If --execute replays a stale list: a 404 on a now-absent ID could abort a non-resumable run unless explicitly handled; a newly-duplicated comment would be missed.
findings.finding: The plan never specifies whether --execute re-fetches live comment state from Jira or replays the dry-run snapshot. If --execute replays the stale dry-run ID list (computed hours/days earlier after human review), comment IDs that were deleted between phases return 404, and comment IDs for new bot duplicates that appeared after dry-run are missed. Without specifying that 404s are silently skipped and that the delete set is recomputed at execute-time, the mechanism is ambiguous and one outcome leaves the purge incomplete or abortable mid-ticket.
findings.location: What section — 'Dry-run FIRST: emit the exact per-ticket delete list (comment ids + kept canonical) and show the diff for human approval before --execute'
scenarios: Operator runs dry-run, waits 12 hours for review, then runs --execute; a comment was manually deleted in Jira by an admin in the interim — script hits 404, aborts, leaves ticket partially cleaned.
An edge-case bot retry between phases posts a new duplicate; --execute's stale list misses it, leaving a fresh duplicate post-cleanup.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `--execute` description: 'At execution time, re-fetch all comments for the ticket (paginated), recompute the bot-only keep-oldest delete set, diff against the approved dry-run list (flag unexpected divergence > N as a warning), then DELETE. HTTP 404 on a DELETE = already deleted, skip and log. HTTP 429 = wait Retry-After seconds and retry.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a pilot step: before the full run, execute a real (non-dry-run) deletion of 5–10 duplicate comments on the smallest affected ticket (e.g., REB-190) and verify auth, pagination, rate-limit behavior, and per-call latency — then use results to tune the script before the full execution.
evidence: Dry-run 'emit the exact per-ticket delete list … for human approval' — emits the list but makes no actual API calls
Jira pagination behavior for fetching all 5000 comments (page size, offset handling) is asserted, not probed
Auth scope for DELETE on bridge-bot comments at this volume is assumed, not tested
No mention of a trial deletion on 1 ticket (e.g., the smallest — REB-190 with 22 distinct/~4978 dupes) before full run
findings.finding: The plan has no small-scale pilot step to empirically validate Jira REST API behavior (pagination at 5000 comments, DELETE latency, rate-limit thresholds) before executing the full ~63,000-deletion run. The dry-run validates dedup logic but does NOT exercise actual API calls at scale, so the operational assumptions (API reachable, auth token scoped for DELETE, pagination works correctly at the 5000-comment boundary) are unverified until the live run begins.
findings.location: Sequencing / What section — no pilot step between dry-run and --execute
scenarios: Auth token lacks DELETE permission — discovered only after dry-run approval, blocking the entire run
Pagination fetches only first 1000 comments, silently leaving 4000 duplicates unfetched — dedup operates on incomplete data
Latency per DELETE is 2 s → 63,000 calls ≈ 35 hours; operator discovers this only mid-run
findings.suggested_fix: Insert a 'pilot' phase in the runbook: select the smallest ticket (REB-190 or REB-426, 22 distinct comments), fetch its full comment list, verify pagination returns all ~5000, delete 10 known duplicates, confirm they disappear in Jira, measure per-call latency, check for 429s — approve full run only after the pilot succeeds.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that --execute re-fetches the live comment list from Jira (rather than replaying the dry-run snapshot) and re-applies the dedup logic, so the delete set is always computed against current state at execution time.
evidence: 'Dry-run FIRST: emit the exact per-ticket delete list (comment ids + kept canonical) and show the diff for human approval before --execute'
Plan does not state whether --execute re-validates/re-fetches comments from Jira at execution time or replays the dry-run snapshot.
P1 stops new bot duplication, but human activity or an edge-case bot retry between phases could alter the comment set.
findings.finding: TOCTOU between dry-run snapshot and --execute: the delete list (comment ids to remove) is computed from a point-in-time Jira fetch during dry-run, but --execute may run arbitrarily later. If comments are added or (in an edge case) if the bot posts between the two phases, the script operates on a stale id list — potentially deleting a comment id that no longer exists (harmless 404) or missing a newly-duplicated comment that should be purged. The plan does not specify whether --execute re-fetches the live comment list or reuses the dry-run output.
findings.location: What — 'Dry-run FIRST: emit the exact per-ticket delete list … for human approval before --execute'
scenarios: Operator runs dry-run, waits for review (hours), then runs --execute; in the interim a new bot comment lands (edge case) or a comment id was deleted by another process — script either skips a duplicate or emits a 404 error that could abort a non-resumable run.
findings.suggested_fix: At --execute time, re-fetch all comments for each ticket from the Jira API, recompute the delete set using the same keep-oldest/bot-only logic, then diff against the dry-run list and surface any additions/removals for operator awareness before proceeding. Treat 404 on an individual delete as a non-fatal logged skip (already gone) so resumability is preserved.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify commit a6cdad0e2 exists in the main branch history (e.g. `git log --oneline main | grep a6cdad0e2`) and add that verification step to the Sequencing gate, since the hash does not appear in any tracked repo file and cannot be confirmed from the plan alone.
evidence: Search for 'a6cdad0e2' across the entire repo returns no matches — the commit hash is not referenced in any tracked file, changelog, or comment.
The plan's sequencing gate says 'confirm via a green reconcile-bridge run on a post-a6cdad0e2 commit' — an unverifiable prerequisite if the hash itself cannot be confirmed.
The dedup fix (truncation guard) IS in outbound_comments.py:248-263, but whether it was introduced by specifically commit a6cdad0e2 cannot be confirmed from the repo files.
findings.finding: The plan's sequencing gate rests on an unverifiable commit-hash assertion: 'P1 (landed a6cdad0e2) STOPS new duplication' — the commit hash a6cdad0e2 does not appear anywhere in the repo's tracked files, so it cannot be confirmed that this specific commit contains the truncation-guard fix OR that it is actually on the main branch as of the plan's execution window. If the hash is wrong or the fix landed in a different commit, the guard against re-inflation is broken.
findings.location: Why section / Sequencing (CRITICAL) — 'P1 (landed a6cdad0e2) STOPS new duplication'
scenarios: The hash is a typo or refers to a commit on a branch not yet merged — the fix is not live and the cleanup script re-inflates.
The operator confirms a 'green reconcile-bridge run' against the wrong baseline, believing P1 is active when it is not.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit pre-flight check: `git merge-base --is-ancestor a6cdad0e2 HEAD` (or equivalent) as the first step of the Sequencing gate, before the green-run confirmation, so the operator can confirm the fix commit is actually present.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether the navasage reconcile-bridge deployment uses `pip install .` (installs from HEAD) or a pinned `pip install nava-rebar==<version>`, and if pinned, confirm the pinned version includes commit a6cdad0e2 before treating a green run as proof that P1 is active.
evidence: reconcile-bridge.yml:136-142: 'TEMPLATE NOTE: client projects use `pip install nava-rebar==<pinned>`. This repo IS rebar, so it installs the checked-out source to reconcile against the code on main.'
docs/jira-sync-setup.md:136: 'Pin a released version (`pip install nava-rebar==X.Y.Z`) so the reconciler code is [pinned]'
The plan's universal assertion ('the reconcile-bridge installs from main each run') is true only for the template repo's own workflow, not necessarily the navasage/REB deployment.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the reconcile-bridge installs from main each run' as the mechanism by which P1 reaches the reconciler 'within minutes of landing.' This is only true for this repo's own template instance (which uses `pip install .` from the checked-out source); the actual navasage deployment may use a pinned `pip install nava-rebar==<pinned>` as recommended in the TEMPLATE NOTE. If the navasage deployment pins a version that predates a6cdad0e2, P1 does NOT reach it automatically, and the sequencing gate is broken.
findings.location: Sequencing (CRITICAL) — 'the reconcile-bridge installs from main each run, so P1 reaches it within minutes of landing'
scenarios: The navasage project uses a pinned nava-rebar version; the operator sees a green reconcile-bridge run but that run is using the pre-P1 code — deletion proceeds and re-inflation happens immediately.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Re-validate at --execute time (not just dry-run time) that each comment id scheduled for deletion still exists, is still a duplicate (≥2 copies of its normalized body remain), and is not the canonical 'keep-oldest' copy before issuing the DELETE call.
evidence: 'emit the exact per-ticket delete list (comment ids + kept canonical)' — list is fixed at dry-run time
P1 reconciler is live and actively posting during the approval window
'only delete bridge-bot-authored comments' — new legitimate posts from bot arrive during approval gap
findings.finding: The delete list is computed and human-approved at dry-run time, but execution (--execute) happens later. Any new bridge-bot comment posted by the reconciler between dry-run and execute that shares a body with a formerly-duplicated comment could be on the pre-computed delete list and incorrectly deleted, since the 'NEVER delete a body with only one copy' guard was evaluated at dry-run time, not at execution time.
findings.location: What — 'Dry-run FIRST: emit the exact per-ticket delete list … for human approval before --execute'
scenarios: Operator approves dry-run output; reconciler posts a new comment on REB-155 during the 30-minute review; that comment's id was not on the dry-run list but its body hash matches a formerly-duplicated body — the execute step deletes it because it iterates the pre-computed id list
Alternatively, a comment scheduled for deletion is the last remaining copy at execute time (all sibling duplicates were deleted earlier in the same run), yet it is still deleted
findings.suggested_fix: At execute time, re-fetch the current comment list for each ticket and diff against the dry-run plan; abort/warn on any deviation (new comments arrived, counts changed) and require re-approval before proceeding.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare in the What/Auth section that HTTP client debug logging is disabled (or Authorization headers are redacted) during execution, and that retained artifacts (backup JSON, deletion log) are verified to contain no credential material before storage.
evidence: 'Auth: JIRA_API_TOKEN in env; acli authenticated to navasage.atlassian.net'
'log each deletion (ticket, comment id, body hash)' — log schema is defined but no mention of header/env-var redaction
'Deletion log artifact retained' — artifact is retained, so any inadvertent token capture persists
ABSENCE: no statement that HTTP client verbose/debug output or error stack traces are suppressed or redacted before artifact retention
findings.finding: The plan specifies that JIRA_API_TOKEN is sourced from the environment and used on the outbound API call path, but does not declare that the token will be suppressed from verbose HTTP logs, error traces, or the backup/deletion-log artifacts. If the execution framework or HTTP client logs request headers or env vars on error, the token could be captured in retained artifacts.
findings.location: What section — 'Auth: JIRA_API_TOKEN in env'; AC — 'Deletion log artifact retained'
scenarios: Script runs with an HTTP-debug flag or the underlying library logs Authorization headers; the retained deletion-log artifact captures the bearer token; a later reader of the artifact obtains valid Jira credentials.
An unhandled exception prints the full environment or request context to stdout/stderr, which is captured in the CI/CD run log and retained alongside the backup artifact.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Auth step: 'Ensure HTTP_DEBUG / verbose logging is off (or Authorization headers are masked) for the run; after execution, confirm neither the backup artifact nor the deletion log contains the string "Bearer" or the token value before archiving.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
